scope: Brazil tactical and coaching team_profile only
missing_fields: set-piece taker and aerial target map
final role confirmation under Carlo Ancelotti

source_log.note: Local context exists but includes malformed JSON/encoding damage in current copy.
Fixture, manager-context, and source trace reference.

notes: No score prediction, group projection, champion projection, or betting advice.

coach_profile.summary: Brazil's ceiling is driven by elite front-line talent, but the tactical question is balance: how much freedom can the wide stars receive without exposing the midfield screen and defensive transition structure.

possession_structure.summary: Brazil should still be most dangerous when possession creates isolation for wide attackers rather than when it becomes slow circulation for its own sake.
possession_structure.build_up: Back four base with one or two midfielders responsible for tempo control and early progression into wide or half-space receivers.

pressing_profile.summary: Expect selective aggression rather than nonstop pressing. Brazil can jump on loose touches and trap near the touchline, but game-state management should matter.

defensive_block.summary: Brazil's settled defense can look strong if the midfield screen protects the center-backs, but fullback spaces and rest-defense spacing remain the stress points.

attacking_transition.summary: This is one of Brazil's clearest elite phases. If the first release finds Vinicius, Rodrygo, or another runner in stride, they can turn open grass into immediate shot threat.

defensive_transition.summary: The main risk comes after Brazil lose the ball with several attackers ahead of it. Recovery structure is more important than individual defending alone.

set_piece_attack.summary: Brazil have enough delivery quality and size to remain dangerous, but the full taker-target hierarchy still needs confirmation.

set_piece_defense.summary: Goalkeeper level and center-back quality suggest a good floor, though the final verdict depends on lineup-specific marking assignments.

game_state_behaviour.leading: Brazil can become more pragmatic once ahead, preferring control and spacing over constant risk.
game_state_behaviour.trailing: When chasing, the shape can become more wing-heavy and talent-driven, with more bodies committed ahead of the ball.

substitution_tendency.summary: Bench depth should allow role-for-role refreshes without a major quality drop, but the exact attacking mix may change based on which creator or runner is protected.

key_player_roles.note: The clearest one-v-one destabilizer and transition accelerator.
Important because he can link attacks instead of only attacking the last line.
Provides right-side width, delivery, and finishing threat.
Defines the floor when the match turns transitional.

tactical_risks: space left around or behind the fullbacks
midfield coverage if the game becomes stretched
overreliance on individual solutions against deep blocks

first_matchday_adjustment_watch.summary: Track whether Brazil protect midfield balance first or lean immediately into maximum attacking width. That choice will shape both defensive floor and totals profile for later matches.

data_gaps: malformed local team packet limits clean structured extraction
need final medical and suspension cross-check

confidence_notes.reason: Supported by repeated player-role logic and squad talent concentration.
Manager identity is clear, but final tournament usage still needs lineup-backed repetition.
Current local material does not provide a clean taker-target map.

market_relevant_flags: Brazil can create a high shot ceiling without needing sustained pressing
If midfield balance weakens, both opponent counters and totals risk rise
Against deep blocks, finishing efficiency matters more than raw possession share

challenge_points: Are we overstating structure because of the coach name rather than lineup evidence?
Does Brazil still need a true control midfielder to avoid chaotic game states?
Are deep-block creation concerns being hidden by star power assumptions?
